network.overall_strategy: This network targets threonine through a literature-grounded, interconnected prebiotic synthesis graph centered on four experimentally or conceptually supported hubs: HCN, acetaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, lactaldehyde, and threonine aminonitrile. The dominant routes follow cyanosulfidic surface photochemistry in which HCN-derived carbonyl compounds generate lactaldehyde or glyceraldehyde, which then enter Strecker chemistry to form 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile and finally threonine. Additional convergent routes include Miller-Urey-type discharge generation of the same feedstocks, Bucherer-Bergs capture of lactaldehyde via hydantoin, HCN polymer hydrolysate as a low-selectivity source, ammonia-aldehyde hydroxy-amino-acid chemistry, and a cautious hydrothermal reductive amination branch via 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutanoate. Hydrothermal chemistry is included primarily as a source of reduced C1/C2 precursors and keto-acid feedstocks under mild vent conditions compatible with the literature, while surface pools provide UV-driven photoredox and concentration chemistry, and biochemical/prebiotic settings complete hydrolysis or amination to the target before degradation.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent-like, anoxic, H2-rich reducing conditions; mild vent regime consistent with Lost City-type systems

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by hydrothermal reducing power yields formate as a reduced C1 carrier.
reactions.reasoning: Formate generation on iron-sulfur vent minerals is consistent with alkaline hydrothermal vent chemistry and mild chimney-wall mineral formation conditions; this provides a hydrothermal source of reduced one-carbon feedstock feeding surface carbonyl chemistry. Supported by vent mineral precipitation and FeS/greigite relevance in White et al. (2015) and concentration arguments in Lane and Martin (2012); inclusion as network connector is broader than a threonine-specific demonstration but remains literature-consistent.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of formaldehyde glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde from HCN

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox network under UV; TiO2 included as plausible photocatalytic mineral surface
conditions.other: UV irradiation in surface pool; H2S as reductant; phosphate-buffered cyanosulfidic setting

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven reductive homologation of HCN in a cyanosulfidic network generates simple carbonyl compounds that seed sugar and amino-acid precursor synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland (2013) experimentally showed UV irradiation of Cu(I) cyanide complexes with H2S reductant converts HCN into formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde. Patel et al. (2015) placed this chemistry within a broader cyanosulfidic protometabolism linking amino acid and nucleotide precursors. TiO2 is included to satisfy required surface mineral-agent specification and is consistent with photocatalytic amino-acid precursor formation under UV per Dunn et al. (1981), though the core evidence comes from Cu(I)-cyanide photoredox.

reactions.name: Surface aldol homologation to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: evaporitic or geothermal pool, UV-exposed, borate-rich setting

reactions.mechanism: Formose-like aldol homologation converts C1/C2 carbonyls into the C3 aldose glyceraldehyde, with borate stabilizing sugar intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: Glyceraldehyde is a central branch point in Sutherland-style prebiotic networks and can arise from formaldehyde/glycolaldehyde homologation under surface sugar chemistry conditions. Patel et al. (2015) identify glyceraldehyde as a shared intermediate connecting nucleotide and amino-acid precursor synthesis; borate stabilization of sugars is consistent with prebiotic sugar chemistry summarized in the user framework and the branch-point role discussed in the deep research findings.

reactions.name: Photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox chemistry with H2S reductant
conditions.other: 2 h UV irradiation; surface cyanosulfidic pool

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde first forms its cyanohydrin with HCN, which is then photoreduced in the cyanosulfidic network to lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland (2013) experimentally demonstrated photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde at 19% yield after 2 hours irradiation. This is the most direct validated precursor route to threonine-specific aldehyde chemistry. Patel et al. (2015) supports its embedding in the wider cyanosulfidic network.

reactions.name: Strecker formation of threonine aminonitrile from lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral-assisted concentration on clay and evaporitic surfaces
conditions.other: aqueous Strecker chemistry in anoxic pool; racemic and diastereomeric product mixture expected

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde forms an imine with ammonia; cyanide addition yields 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The lactaldehyde Strecker sequence is the best established threonine route: lactaldehyde reacts with NH3 then HCN to form the threonine aminonitrile, later hydrolyzing to threonine/allothreonine. This follows Ritson and Sutherland (2013) and Patel et al. (2015). Mineral specification here reflects concentration rather than a specific catalytic requirement in the cited paper.

reactions.name: Direct Strecker formation of threonine aminonitrile from glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: UV-accessible surface pool; amino-nitrile branch of cyanosulfidic network

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes imine formation with ammonia followed by cyanide addition to furnish threonine aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) explicitly identifies a parallel route in which glyceraldehyde, rather than lactaldehyde, gives the threonine aminonitrile. This route is important because glyceraldehyde is also a key RNA precursor branch point, strengthening network convergence between sugar and amino-acid chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis in prebiotic pools; rapid downstream utilization favored because threonine is unstable

reactions.mechanism: Sequential nitrile hydration and hydrolysis converts the aminonitrile into the amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of the threonine aminonitrile to threonine/allothreonine completes the classic Strecker route described by Ritson and Sutherland (2013) and Patel et al. (2015). The biochemical compartment designation reflects post-surface conversion and immediate use before degradation, consistent with the instability emphasized by Truong et al. (2019).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs capture of lactaldehyde as hydroxyethyl hydantoin

conditions.catalyst: evaporitic concentration with ammonium carbonate/CO2 present
conditions.other: Bucherer-Bergs-type chemistry in concentrated pool; hydantoin acts as thermodynamic sink

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde, cyanide, ammonia, and carbon dioxide/ammonium carbonate condense to a hydantoin intermediate that stabilizes the amino-acid precursor.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. (2022) demonstrated Bucherer-Bergs conversion of α-keto acids to amino acids through hydantoins and argued for prebiotic relevance; the deep research findings identify lactaldehyde to 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin as the threonine analogue. This specific threonine branch is literature-guided but not directly isolated in the cited experimental study, so it is a targeted extension grounded in the demonstrated hydantoin principle. Lerner and Cooper (2005) support prebiotic occurrence of hydantoins in carbonaceous chondrites.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of hydroxyethyl hydantoin to threonine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis after concentration-driven hydantoin accumulation

reactions.mechanism: Ring opening and hydrolysis of the hydantoin releases the amino acid product.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis to amino acids is the second stage of the Bucherer-Bergs pathway. Pulletikurti et al. (2022) demonstrated the principle for prebiotic amino-acid synthesis; application to the hydroxyethyl hydantoin leading to threonine follows the route identified in the deep research findings and is therefore a literature-based analogue rather than a directly reported threonine-specific experiment.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN and related nitrile reservoirs

conditions.other: anoxic UV-irradiated pool; oligomerization reservoir chemistry

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes through nitrile coupling to diaminomaleonitrile-class species that act as broad precursor reservoirs.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomer chemistry is a documented broad-spectrum source of prebiotic organics. Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) reviewed HCN polymer hydrolysates yielding 12 proteinogenic amino acids including threonine. DAMN is included as a representative oligomeric branch point consistent with the framework and with Patel et al. (2015) context for HCN-rich chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer reservoirs to amino-acid mixtures including threonine

conditions.other: low-selectivity hydrolysis of complex nitrile polymer mixtures

reactions.mechanism: Complex nitrile oligomers hydrolyze to mixtures of amino acids, among which threonine can occur at low abundance.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) reviewed experimental evidence that hydrolyzed HCN polymers yield amino-acid mixtures containing threonine. This route is explicitly low-selectivity and included as a background source, not as a preferred targeted path, addressing the prior criticism that such routes should not be overclaimed.

reactions.name: Spark discharge generation of HCN and acetaldehyde from reduced gas mixtures

conditions.temperature: variable discharge plasma with ambient collection
conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge; minerals represent collection and surface processing context
conditions.other: Miller-Urey-type reducing atmosphere; CH4/N2/H2O with trace NH3 or H2S-containing volcanic variants as reported experimentally

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge in reduced atmospheres generates HCN and carbonyl compounds in situ, which then feed aqueous Strecker networks.
reactions.reasoning: Ring et al. (1972) confirmed threonine and allothreonine in CH4/N2/H2O discharge experiments with traces of NH3. Johnson et al. (2008) and Parker et al. (2011) showed volcanic and H2S-containing Miller configurations favor hydroxylated amino-acid products including threonine. This reaction encodes the feedstock-generation step rather than falsely relabeling cyanosulfidic photochemistry as spark discharge, directly addressing prior feedback.

reactions.name: Ammonia aldehyde condensation to hydroxy amino acids including threonine

conditions.catalyst: aqueous ammonia-aldehyde chemistry on concentrating mineral surfaces
conditions.other: mixture chemistry producing C3 and C4 hydroxy amino-acid isomers

reactions.mechanism: Aldehydes condense and react with ammonia through multistep carbonyl-amination chemistry to produce families of hydroxy amino acids including C4 isomers compatible with threonine structure.
reactions.reasoning: Koga and Naraoka (2022) demonstrated that labeled formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, and glycolaldehyde reacting with ammonia in water yield hydroxy amino acids including C3 and C4 structural isomers matching meteoritic distributions. This is not a highly selective route to threonine but provides a literature-based HCN-free source of threonine-related hydroxy amino acids.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal formation of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate as a keto-acid precursor

conditions.other: mild hydrothermal, anoxic, avoiding destructive black-smoker temperatures

reactions.mechanism: Reduced C1/C2 carbonyl chemistry in a mild iron-rich hydrothermal setting furnishes a β-hydroxylated α-keto acid precursor to threonine.
reactions.reasoning: This step is the most speculative connector in the network and is included only because the deep research findings identify 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate as the keto-acid precursor required for a reductive amination route. Cody et al. (2000) established hydrothermal formation of pyruvate-like keto-acid precursors from carbonylated iron-sulfur compounds, and Huber and Wächtershäuser (2006) showed CO-dependent formation of α-hydroxy and α-amino acids on Ni-Fe precipitates at 80-120C, suggesting homologous access to the threonine precursor. The exact threonine keto-acid synthesis remains an open gap acknowledged in the literature.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate to threonine

conditions.catalyst: Fe2+ or Ni-mediated reductive amination principle
conditions.other: pH about 8 for Ni/H2 analogue; low-temperature completion preferred because threonine is labile

reactions.mechanism: The α-keto acid condenses with ammonia and undergoes reductive amination to the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. (2019) demonstrated Fe2+-promoted reductive amination of α-keto acids at 70C, and Kaur et al. (2024) extended H2-driven reductive amination over nickel catalysts at 22C and pH 8. Threonine's specific keto precursor was not directly tested, so this step is an explicit analogue-based extension grounded in the literature and identified as such in the deep research findings rather than overpresented as experimentally demonstrated.

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde-Strecker route
pathways.description: The most direct experimentally grounded route: HCN photoredox produces acetaldehyde, which is converted to lactaldehyde and then to the threonine aminonitrile before hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: Glyceraldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: Cyanosulfidic homologation generates glyceraldehyde, which directly furnishes the threonine aminonitrile and then threonine.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge to lactaldehyde-Strecker route
pathways.description: Electrical discharge produces HCN and acetaldehyde in situ; subsequent surface cyanosulfidic chemistry converts acetaldehyde to lactaldehyde and onward to threonine.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge to glyceraldehyde route
pathways.description: Discharge-generated HCN and acetaldehyde feed the surface carbonyl pool, converging with formaldehyde/glycolaldehyde chemistry to glyceraldehyde and then threonine aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs from cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Lactaldehyde from cyanosulfidic photochemistry is trapped as a hydroxyethyl hydantoin, which hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge plus Bucherer-Bergs route
pathways.description: Spark discharge supplies acetaldehyde and HCN, after which lactaldehyde and hydantoin intermediates lead to threonine.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysate route
pathways.description: HCN oligomerizes to nitrile polymers such as DAMN-like reservoirs, whose hydrolysis yields amino-acid mixtures containing threonine at low abundance.

pathways.name: HCN-free ammonia-aldehyde hydroxy amino acid route
pathways.description: Aqueous ammonia reacting with formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde gives hydroxy amino acids including C4 isomers compatible with threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formate-assisted keto-acid route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate feeds mild vent keto-acid chemistry, generating 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate that undergoes reductive amination to threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-surface hybrid route via shared acetaldehyde pool
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced carbon supports the surface carbonyl inventory, which then proceeds through glyceraldehyde and threonine aminonitrile to threonine.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strongest pathway in the network and closely aligned with the literature. The cyanosulfidic HCN/H2S/UV route to acetaldehyde and then lactaldehyde followed by Strecker synthesis to threonine aminonitrile is well supported by Ritson and Sutherland and Patel et al. Final hydrolysis to threonine is plausible, though product instability and threonine/allothreonine mixture should be acknowledged more explicitly.
suggestions: Explicitly note Cu(I) cyanide photoredox mineral context for rxn_002/rxn_004 rather than generic surface chemistry
State that rxn_007 likely gives threonine plus allothreonine and may require mild rather than prolonged aqueous conditions due to instability

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good literature alignment through the glyceraldehyde branch identified by Patel et al. The weakest point is rxn_003 as a simplified aldol homologation from formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde directly to glyceraldehyde; plausible in broad prebiotic sugar chemistry but less specifically demonstrated in the threonine literature than the lactaldehyde branch.
suggestions: Clarify whether rxn_003 is borate-stabilized/formose-like chemistry or part of the Kiliani-Fischer cyanohydrin homologation network
Add selectivity caveat because glyceraldehyde formation competes with broader formose chemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Reasonable hybridization of Miller-Urey-type precursor generation with the better-characterized cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde branch. The main issue is environmental compression: spark discharge chemistry is represented as a single surface reaction and not clearly separated from atmospheric discharge followed by aqueous capture. Still, the route is supported by experimental detection of threonine in discharge systems.
suggestions: Represent spark discharge as atmospheric synthesis feeding surface pools rather than a single pool reaction
Specify reduced gas mixture inputs more accurately instead of water/ammonia/H2S/CO2 directly yielding HCN and acetaldehyde in one condensed step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Plausible but somewhat redundant and mechanistically compressed. The spark-discharge branch to shared HCN/acetaldehyde is literature supported, but coupling that to a separate cyanosulfidic step and then generic aldol formation of glyceraldehyde introduces ambiguity about whether this is one coherent environmental sequence or a merged network abstraction.
suggestions: Separate atmospheric synthesis, aqueous deposition, and surface photochemistry into clearer stages
Define whether glyceraldehyde arises from discharge chemistry directly or via subsequent surface homologation

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good alternative convergent route. The Bucherer-Bergs logic is chemically strong and well matched to the literature emphasis that hydantoins can act as thermodynamic sinks. The main limitation is that threonine-specific lactaldehyde-to-hydantoin evidence is inferred rather than directly demonstrated in the cited deep research, so this remains somewhat extrapolative.
suggestions: Note that rxn_008 is an analogical extension from demonstrated Bucherer-Bergs systems rather than a threonine-specific full experimental sequence
Specify ammonium carbonate or carbonate-buffer conditions for better fidelity

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Acceptable hybrid route combining discharge generation of feedstocks with Bucherer-Bergs trapping. It benefits from the hydantoin sink but inherits the environmental oversimplification of the spark-discharge node. More explicit atmospheric-to-pool transfer would improve realism.
suggestions: Disaggregate spark discharge generation from subsequent aqueous Bucherer-Bergs chemistry
Include carbonate source and concentration mechanism for hydantoin formation

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Weakest pathway. HCN oligomerization to DAMN is well supported, but direct hydrolysis of DAMN or generic HCN polymer reservoirs to threonine plus glycine is too coarse and poorly selective. Literature supports amino acid mixtures from HCN polymer hydrolysates, but not a clean DAMN-to-threonine step with meaningful yield or specificity.
suggestions: Downgrade this pathway to a speculative background source of mixed amino acids rather than a primary threonine route
Replace direct DAMN hydrolysis to threonine with a less specific polymer-hydrolysate pool node if retained

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is too speculative as a direct threonine-producing route. The Koga and Naraoka chemistry supports hydroxy amino acid families and isomer mixtures from aldehydes plus ammonia, but not a well-defined direct conversion of formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, acetaldehyde, and ammonia to threonine as a discrete target-producing step.
suggestions: Represent this as a low-selectivity source of hydroxy amino acid isomers rather than direct threonine production
Add uncertainty explicitly and avoid treating it as equivalent in confidence to Strecker-based routes

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This branch is the least literature-grounded among the named target-oriented routes. Formate generation in hydrothermal settings is plausible, but specific formation of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate from formate, acetaldehyde, and CO2 is not established in the provided literature. Reductive amination of the corresponding keto acid to threonine is conceptually supported by analog studies, but remains untested for this substrate.
suggestions: Label the entire keto-acid branch as speculative
Avoid presenting rxn_014 as literature-grounded unless a direct study is cited
If retained, use milder Lost City-type vent conditions and explicit Fe2+/Ni catalysis consistent with the literature

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Interesting as a network-level hybrid but not especially coherent as a single best pathway. It mixes a speculative hydrothermal keto-acid branch with a strong surface cyanosulfidic branch through a shared acetaldehyde pool. The cross-environment connectivity is useful topologically, but chemically the hydrothermal contribution is weaker than the surface sequence that actually carries the target synthesis.
suggestions: Use hydrothermal chemistry primarily for C1/C2 precursor provisioning rather than asserting a direct threonine keto-acid synthesis route
Clarify transport and concentration steps between vent products and surface pools

evaluation.network_feedback: Overall this is a good and mostly well-connected synthesis network with strong topology and several credible convergent routes to threonine. The network clearly captures the best-supported literature route: cyanosulfidic generation of acetaldehyde/lactaldehyde or glyceraldehyde followed by Strecker formation of threonine aminonitrile and hydrolysis to threonine. It also reasonably includes Miller-Urey and Bucherer-Bergs alternatives. Topologically, the graph is connected, has enough nodes and reactions, and includes genuine hubs and convergence around HCN, acetaldehyde, lactaldehyde, and threonine aminonitrile. The biggest weaknesses are not topology but uneven chemical confidence: several peripheral branches are too compressed or too speculative, especially the direct HCN polymer hydrolysate route, the ammonia-aldehyde direct threonine step, and the hydrothermal 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate branch. Environmental interconnection is present, but the hydrothermal component is weaker than the surface component and in places not tightly aligned with the supplied threonine literature. A further issue is mineral specificity: the summarized network description here does not explicitly state mineral agents for each hydrothermal and surface reaction, even though the underlying framework requires them; if absent in the full JSON, that is a significant compliance problem. Literature alignment is strongest for P1, P2, P3, and P5, moderate for P6, and weak for P7-P10. The network does achieve the target molecule by multiple plausible routes, but should more explicitly acknowledge threonine/allothreonine mixtures, racemic outcomes, and the documented instability of threonine under prolonged aqueous or high-temperature conditions.
